description:
You are a script developer who needs to place a small bundle of utilities under tight, shared-group control.  
Three helper scripts already exist inside the directory “/home/user/dev_utils”, but right now both the directory and the scripts have overly-permissive default permissions.  

Your job:  
1. Harden the directory and the scripts with a single, compound terminal command of your choice (for example by chaining commands with “&&” or a semicolon).  
2. Generate a four-line permissions report so that our automated tests can confirm your work.

Detailed technical requirements
A. Permissions that must be in force at the end of the task  
   1. Directory “/home/user/dev_utils”  
      • Final permission bits: 2750  
          – read, write, execute for owner;  
          – read, execute for group;  
          – no rights for others;  
          – set-gid bit ON so anything later created inside inherits the directory’s group.  
   2. Script files residing in that directory  
      • build.sh, test.sh and deploy.sh must each end up as 750 (rwxr-x---).  

B. Verification report (MUST be created)  
   • Path: “/home/user/dev_utils/perm_report.txt”  
   • Exactly four lines, terminated with a single “\n” each (no extra blank line at the end).  
   • Fixed column order and spelling:  

     Line 1 DIR /home/user/dev_utils : <octal>  
     Line 2 FILE build.sh : <octal>  
     Line 3 FILE test.sh : <octal>  
     Line 4 FILE deploy.sh : <octal>  

     where “<octal>” is the three- or four-digit numeric mode (e.g. 2750, 750) captured *after* you have applied the requested changes.  
   • Every line begins with either “DIR” or “FILE”, followed by a single space, then the exact path (no trailing slash), a single space, a colon, another single space, and finally the numeric permissions.  
   • Example of the exact formatting style (numbers here are only illustrative):  
     DIR /home/user/dev_utils : 2750  

C. Constraints  
   • Accomplish both sub-tasks (permission changes + report creation) via *one* compound terminal command.  
   • You do not have root privileges, so rely solely on standard user-level utilities (chmod, stat, printf, echo, etc.).  
   • Do not modify the contents of the three shell scripts—only their mode bits.  
   • Any additional temporary files you create must be cleaned up or placed outside “/home/user/dev_utils”.  
   • When you are done, “/home/user/dev_utils/perm_report.txt” must be the only new file inside that directory.

Clarifications  
• “Single, compound command” means that in the terminal you must press “ENTER” only once; you are however free to concatenate several commands with semicolons “;” or logical operators such as “&&”.  
• The automated checker will:  
  1. Inspect the mode of “/home/user/dev_utils”.  
  2. Inspect the mode of the three *.sh files.  
  3. Read and parse “perm_report.txt” line by line, verifying both the syntax and the numeric modes.  

If everything matches the specification, the task is complete.
truth:
Initial system state BEFORE the agent starts  
1. “/home/user/dev_utils” exists and is owned by user “user”, primary group “user”.  
   • Initial permission bits: 755 (rwxr-xr-x).  
2. Inside that directory:  
   • build.sh  – contents: “#!/bin/bash\necho 'build'\n”  
   • test.sh   – contents: “#!/bin/bash\necho 'test'\n”  
   • deploy.sh  – contents: “#!/bin/bash\necho 'deploy'\n”  
   • All three scripts initially have mode 644 (rw-r--r--).  

Expected FINAL state after correct task execution  
1. Directory “/home/user/dev_utils” : mode 2750.  
2. Files build.sh, test.sh, deploy.sh : each mode 750.  
3. A new file “/home/user/dev_utils/perm_report.txt” must exist with the exact 4-line content:

DIR /home/user/dev_utils : 2750
FILE build.sh : 750
FILE test.sh : 750
FILE deploy.sh : 750

(no blank line after the last row, each line terminated by a single newline character).

The automated test will compare the actual numeric modes obtained via “stat -c '%a'” against the lines recorded in perm_report.txt and will confirm that both the filesystem and the report agree with the specification.
